questions.prompt: Complete: "I enjoy ___ music late at night."
questions.explanation: "enjoy" is a gerund-only verb — it must be followed by verb + -ing: I enjoy listening. "to listen" (infinitive) cannot follow enjoy, "listen" is the bare base form, and "listened" is the past tense.
"decide" is an infinitive-only verb: She decided to buy. "buying" (gerund) cannot follow decide, "buy" is the bare infinitive without "to", and "bought" is the past tense.

questions.prompt: Which sentence means "She paused what she was doing in order to have a cigarette"?
questions.explanation: "stop to do" means pause an activity in order to do something else: stopped to smoke = paused in order to smoke. "stopped smoking" means she quit smoking permanently. "quit to smoke" is not a natural English construction, and "gave up smoking" also means she ceased permanently.

questions.prompt: Complete: "I'm looking forward ___ you next week."
questions.explanation: In "look forward to", the word "to" is a preposition, not an infinitive marker, so it must be followed by a gerund: look forward to seeing. "to see" treats "to" as an infinitive marker (wrong here), and "seeing" alone is missing the required preposition "to".

options.text: I remember posting the letter — I was at the post office at noon.
Please remember posting the letter before you leave.
She avoided making eye contact during the interview.
They agreed to meet outside the cinema.

questions.explanation: Sentence (b) is incorrect. "Remember to do" (infinitive) means don't forget a future task, so it should be "remember to post the letter". "Remember doing" (gerund) recalls a past event — but posting the letter hasn't happened yet here. (a), (c) and (d) all follow the correct gerund/infinitive rules.
